---
title: "Probability and Text Generation"
subtitle: "Primer 2 for Applied Generative AI Business Analytics"
number-sections: false
date: "2026-01-01"
date-modified: today
date-format: long
engine: jupyter
bibliography: ../../references.bib
categories: ["M0", "Primer", "Probability", "Text Generation"]
description: "A technical primer explaining next-token probability, softmax, sampling, temperature, and why likelihood is not truth."
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    fontsize: 10pt
    css: primer-style.css
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.primer-kicker}
Module 0 Primer | Points of Significance
:::

# Probability and Text Generation

Large language models generate text by repeatedly estimating a probability
distribution over the next token. The model does not first write a full answer
and then display it. It conditions on the prompt and all tokens already
generated, scores possible next tokens, transforms those scores into
probabilities, selects one token, and repeats.

If a generated response contains tokens \(w_1, \ldots, w_T\), an autoregressive
language model factorizes the probability of the sequence as

$$
\begin{align}
P(w_1, \ldots, w_T)
= \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}).
\end{align}
$$

With a prompt or external context \(c\), the conditional version is

$$
\begin{align}
P(w_1, \ldots, w_T \mid c)
= \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid c, w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}).
\end{align}
$$

::: {.key-box}
**Key idea.** Text generation is conditional probability applied sequentially.
Every generated token changes the context for the next prediction.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-text-generation-probability
#| fig-cap: "Three probability views of text generation. (a) The model assigns logits to candidate next tokens and softmax converts them into probabilities. (b) Temperature changes the sharpness of the distribution, affecting variation in sampled outputs. (c) A generated response is a chain of conditional choices, so early selections influence later probabilities."
#| fig-width: 8.2
#| fig-height: 2.55
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams.update({
    "font.size": 8,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "figure.dpi": 140
})

def softmax(z, temperature=1.0):
    z = np.asarray(z, dtype=float) / temperature
    z = z - z.max()
    exp_z = np.exp(z)
    return exp_z / exp_z.sum()

tokens = ["overdue", "paid", "missing", "due", "incorrect"]
logits = np.array([3.0, 2.4, 1.5, 1.2, 0.7])
prob = softmax(logits)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8.2, 2.55))

# Panel a: logits to probabilities
ax = axes[0]
x = np.arange(len(tokens))
ax.bar(x - 0.18, logits / logits.max(), width=0.34, color="#9ca3af", label="scaled logit")
ax.bar(x + 0.18, prob, width=0.34, color="#2563eb", label="probability")
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(tokens, rotation=35, ha="right")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
ax.set_ylabel("relative value")
ax.set_title("Scores become probabilities", pad=4)
ax.text(-0.45, 0.98, "a", fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=7, loc="upper right")

# Panel b: temperature distributions
ax = axes[1]
temps = [0.4, 1.0, 1.8]
colors = ["#111827", "#2563eb", "#f97316"]
for temp, color in zip(temps, colors):
    ax.plot(x, softmax(logits, temp), marker="o", lw=1.4, color=color, label=f"T={temp}")
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(tokens, rotation=35, ha="right")
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.9)
ax.set_ylabel("next-token probability")
ax.set_title("Temperature controls spread", pad=4)
ax.text(-0.45, 0.83, "b", fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=7)

# Panel c: sequential dependency
ax = axes[2]
steps = np.arange(1, 6)
p_path = np.array([0.47, 0.38, 0.55, 0.31, 0.44])
alt_path = np.array([0.24, 0.33, 0.21, 0.29, 0.18])
ax.plot(steps, p_path, marker="o", color="#047857", lw=1.4, label="chosen path")
ax.plot(steps, alt_path, marker="o", color="#9ca3af", lw=1.2, label="alternative")
ax.set_xticks(steps)
ax.set_xlabel("generation step")
ax.set_ylabel("conditional probability")
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.65)
ax.set_title("Each token changes context", pad=4)
ax.text(0.68, 0.60, "c", fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=7)

fig.tight_layout(w_pad=1.0)
plt.show()
```

**Figure 1a** shows the last step of a neural language model. The model produces
unnormalized scores, called logits, for candidate tokens. The softmax function
turns these scores into probabilities:

$$
\begin{align}
P(w_i \mid c) =
\frac{\exp(z_i)}
{\sum_j \exp(z_j)}.
\end{align}
$$

The highest-scoring token is not guaranteed to be selected unless decoding is
configured greedily. Many systems sample from the distribution, which allows
multiple valid continuations.

**Figure 1b** shows why generation settings matter. Temperature rescales logits
before softmax:

$$
\begin{align}
P_T(w_i \mid c) =
\frac{\exp(z_i/T)}
{\sum_j \exp(z_j/T)}.
\end{align}
$$

Low temperature concentrates probability on the highest-scoring token. High
temperature spreads probability over more candidates. In business analytics,
low-temperature generation is often useful for extraction, classification, and
reporting; higher-temperature generation may be useful for brainstorming or
scenario generation.

**Figure 1c** shows the sequential nature of generation. Once the model chooses
a token, that token becomes part of the context. A weak early choice can shift
later probabilities and make a response drift. This is one reason long
generations need constraints, intermediate checks, retrieval, or structured
outputs.

# Tokens Are the Unit of Prediction

A token is not necessarily a word. It may be a word, part of a word, a number
fragment, punctuation, whitespace, or a code symbol. Tokenization affects:

- how many model steps are needed to generate an answer;
- how much context fits in the context window;
- how names, abbreviations, numbers, and rare terms are represented;
- how much an API call costs.

For example, a phrase such as `quarterly revenue increased by 12%` might be
represented as several tokens: `quarterly`, ` revenue`, ` increased`, ` by`,
` 12`, `%`. The exact decomposition depends on the tokenizer.

# Likelihood, Perplexity and Fluency

Language models are often trained to assign high probability to observed text.
One common training objective is cross-entropy loss, which penalizes the model
when it assigns low probability to the true next token. Perplexity is an
exponentiated version of this loss and can be interpreted as the model's
effective uncertainty over next-token choices.

Lower perplexity means the model is better at predicting text from a given
distribution. It does not necessarily mean the model is more truthful, more
ethical, or more useful for a business decision. A model can assign high
likelihood to a fluent but unsupported statement because the statement resembles
common language patterns.

::: {.pitfall-box}
**Pitfall.** Probability is not truth. A language model estimates plausible
continuations, not verified facts. Factuality must be supplied by evidence,
retrieval, tools, tests, or human review.
:::

# A Worked Analytics Example

Suppose the prompt is:

> The main reason customers delayed renewal was

Without evidence, the model may assign high probability to continuations such
as `budget constraints`, `implementation delays`, or `lack of stakeholder
alignment`, because these are common business explanations. The answer may be
fluent and plausible even if the actual renewal data show a different pattern.

Now suppose the prompt includes retrieved evidence:

> In 37 of 52 renewal notes, customers mentioned unresolved security review.
> In 9 notes, customers mentioned budget. In 6 notes, customers mentioned
> procurement timing. The main reason customers delayed renewal was

The conditional distribution changes. Tokens related to security review should
now receive higher probability because the evidence changes the context. This
is the central logic of retrieval-augmented generation: change the input
context so useful tokens become more probable.

# Decoding Choices and Analytic Tasks

| Task | Desired behavior | Typical decoding preference |
|---|---|---|
| Field extraction | Stable, exact, schema-following | Low temperature, structured output |
| Classification | Consistent labels | Low temperature, constrained labels |
| Summarization | Faithful compression | Low to moderate temperature, evidence included |
| Brainstorming | Diverse alternatives | Higher temperature or multiple samples |
| Report drafting | Fluent but grounded narrative | Moderate temperature with citations/checks |

Decoding settings do not solve reasoning or factuality by themselves. They
control how the model uses its probability distribution. The surrounding system
must still specify the task, provide evidence, constrain the output, and test
the result.

# Mini Exercise

For the prompt:

> The invoice should be flagged because

write five possible next-token continuations. Then identify which continuations
would be acceptable only if supporting data were present. Finally, rewrite the
prompt so the model must condition on invoice fields rather than general
business plausibility.

::: {.takeaway-box}
**Takeaway.** Language models generate text by chaining next-token
probabilities. The quality of a business output depends on how well the system
controls the context, decoding process, evidence, and evaluation.
:::
